speaker: Chuck Freeman (host), tribute to Buck O'Neil
text: Wake up everybody, no more sleeping in bed No more back-to-thinkin' time for thinking ahead World has changed so very much of what it used to be There's so much hatred, more in poverty Oh, oh, oh, oh Wake up for the builders' time to build a new land I know we could do it if we all in the head The only thing we have to do is put it in our minds Surely things will work out, they do it every time The world won't get no better If we're just letting be No, no, no, no, no, no, no The world won't get no better We gotta change it by just you and me Change it yet, change it yet, just you and me Change it yet, change it, go to the low, go to the low Need some help, go, go Go to the low, go to the low, yeah, yeah Wake up, stop the water Wake up, everybody Need a little help, y'all Got some things, need a little help Save more Good afternoon, my friend You are listening to SoulTuck, a spiritual feast to 11 The World Soul I am your sacred chef, Chuck Freeman On KOOP Austin, 91.7 FM SoulTuck's first broadcast in our new Primo studio On today's menu Soul Food, the book starts here The sanctified segue Open your heart to love by Maurice White The hearty fair dinner conversation was Zoe Irene Van Zant And the soul stirrer with embodied faith in action The original SoulTuck guest Jaime Samuelson with his new book Do I End And now the sanctified segue Open your heart to love by Maurice White The hearty fair dinner conversation was over Good afternoon, my friend You are listening to SoulTuck's first broadcast in our new Primo studio Soul Food, the book starts here The sanctified segue Open your heart to love by Maurice White The world is weak for you To leave and celebrate All you dreams come true You never be loved Open your heart to love Open your heart to love by Maurice White The hearty fair dinner conversation was over Good afternoon, my friend Open your heart to love by Maurice White Now, show the world what you feel You know what I'm told You know I'm dead You can find your life You know what I'm told You'll find your love To leave your new baby And all of this is new for you You will do love You will do love You will do love You will do love You are Soul Food for the day If I speak in the tongues of men and of angels But have not love I'm a noisy gong or a clanging symbol And if I have prophetic powers And understand all mysteries And all knowledge And if I have all faith So as to remove mountains But have not love I am nothing Buck O'Neil was born in rural Florida 1911 He was denied the opportunity to attend high school Because his skin was black Buck made some money working on his dad's salary field And moved to Jacksonville, Florida Where one of four black high schools were located He graduated And even completed a couple years of college In 1937 Buck O'Neil signed with the Memphis Red Sox And the newly formed Negro American League And that's baseball for those of you Who are athletically challenged The following year he went to the Kansas City Monarchs Where he played the rest of his career But at a lifetime batting average of 288 Was a four time all star and played in two Negro League World Series He became the first black coach For baseball For major league baseball so-called With the Chicago Cubs In 1962 Signing future Hall of Famer Greats, Lou Brock and Ernie Banks To play for the Chicago Cubs O'Neil gained national prominence And his compelling narration of the Negro leagues And Ken Burns 1994 Baseball documentary Buck led the effort to establish the Negro leagues Baseball Museum in Kansas City Which I really want to go to A buddy of mine went there A couple of months ago I cannot wait to go And has been an ambassador For the Negro leagues and for baseball For many many years This past summer O'Neil missed being chosen To the baseball Hall of Fame by one vote What 17 fellow Negro league players were inducted But Buck was invited to speak At the induction for the Negro league players With this background in mind From Buck's life I want you to listen to this excerpt From his July 29th O'Neil's speech To the baseball Hall of Fame They always said to me, Buck, I know you Hate people For what they did to you Or what they did to your poop I said, no, man, I I never learned to hate I hate cancer Cancer killed my mother My wife died 10 years ago Or cancer I'm singillated A good friend of mine I hate hate a good friend of mine Die of age three months ago I hate ease But I can't hate a human being Because my God never made anything ugly Now you can be ugly if you want a boy But God didn't make it that way Uh-oh I hate hate hate But I can't hate a human being Because my God never made anything ugly Uh-oh So I want you to like this ballet up This afternoon Modern say the coffee is understanding Created A resembled goodwill told all men A coffee is an overprowing love Which seeks nothing in return And when you reach love on this level You love all men Not because you like them Not because their ways appear to you You love them because God loved them And I loved your hope of my God With all my heart, with all my soul And I love every one of you As I love myself Now I want you to do something for me I'm figuring to get off the stage And I think I've done my six minutes But I want you to do something for me I want you to hold hands Whoever's next to you Hold your hand Come on you, all of famous You people out there Hold hands Everybody hooked up Everybody hooked up Well, and I tell you what See, I know my brothers up here My brothers over there I see some black brothers of mine And sisters out there I know they can sing Can you white folks sing I want you to sing after me The greatest thing Come on everybody The greatest thing And all my life Is loving you The greatest thing And all my life Is loving you The greatest thing And all my life Is loving you The greatest thing And all my life Is loving you Thank you folks Thank you folks Thank you folks Thank you Thank you Thank you Thank you Now sit down I can talk to you ten minutes longer But I got to go to the bathroom Seven weeks later After Buck Gave this speech On October 6 He died at the age of 94 Not having realized his Dream of becoming a Hall of Famer Last week, however Buck O'Neill was posh She missly awarded The Presidential Medal of Freedom Our nation's highest civilian honor So faith, hope, love abide These three But the greatest of these Is love The buck Starts Here Open your heart for love this is Solta Kier on KOOP Austin 91.7 FM. I am your host and sacred chef. Chuck Freeman at our new studios on 38th and Airport. Come on by and say howdy with us. Check out. You will not believe if you've ever been to co-op. You will not believe one of the finest studios in Austin, Texas. So we move now to the Hardy Fair portion of our program. That's where we sit around with the soul food. Maybe watch a few dishes and talk about how it went down and happy to have congregant Zoe Irene Van Zat in the studio today. Just don't ask me to sing along with Buck. I'm surprised you could sing that well at that. I had to know that. That's a nice one. That great. So what are your thoughts? You said before we went on the air said I don't know anything about baseball but this isn't about baseball. No it's not. It's about love. I see a lot of love in this studio. Just the outpouring of the community to build this art to create this space. That's really wonderful. Oh yeah. I mean so many people gave of time, money and energy and good hard-edness and hard work. Yeah. It shows. It is wonderful studio. Especially if you've seen the previous homes of KOOP as I have. You saw us on our previous fire trap, right? Yep. Yeah. This is a big improvement. Yeah. Well glad you're able to be a part of it. Thanks. So what do you think about Buck? I mean here's a guy who you know all of his life you know lived under these racist conditions and even I knew the end was slided. But yeah he still has this very positive loving attitude. I wish that under certain or similar circumstances I would be as gracious I'm not sure I would be. Yeah. Well just as you look at your own life to the extent you're able to do that what is it you're pulling from? Is it some bullish system? You know when you are wrong or when you are slided. After the initial hurt. Well you know I try to take the big picture. I try to look at the big picture and I suppose my spiritual life is pretty much grounded in rationalism. So I try to look at it from a rational point of view. Love is not always rational. I know certainly when I've had an experience say of someone I thought we were really good friends and then all of a sudden I'm no longer in their life. That is often a difficult situation and corny as it sounds. I have to remember that little thing about you know some friends are for a reason some friends are for a season etc. Okay so maybe this friend was just for a season. To have that sort of universal love of mankind is difficult. I tend to love people in the concrete and have a hard time with the concept of loving people abstract. You know I love everybody. I don't think I can say that honestly. There are certain people I love but just mankind in general. I'm not at that point yet where I can be that loving. I mean that's something I need to work on. Well one of the things that I took note of there with with the book and that's one of the reasons I read you may have recognized those words from a first Corinthians 13 from your Christian days there is no way I read that. One of the reasons I read that is kind of the top and the bottom of that soul food piece is that he referred to the Yagapai love which is what St. Paul was referring to there that godly unconditional love and I think that distinction that Boko Neal made in his little speech clip there but I may not like him true but I love them. What how do you understand that distinction? You know certainly I see that in the way I say I look at politicians you know without naming names there are some of them that are very difficult to love but you know or to like but you know I try to I try to stick to my principle of well yes everybody has the right to their own opinion and if I have to and I keep telling my students you know we have to watch the tendency humans have to separate us into them and us because that's what causes conflict and horrible things and we have to be able to see that other person as as human whether we like them whether we disagree with them whatever the circumstances is we have to see them as a as another human or else we get into that us in them situation that allows things like slavery and Holocaust and murder and in Darfur and that sort of thing. So so that of course is going to one extreme. I hope that that people can see some glimpse of love of concrete and abstract. I don't I'm not sure everybody can do that you know I'm not sure everybody can open up and and just see what love is is there in their life you know I in the last few months I really have been just tremendous awakening to that it's been quite liberating. One of the things that impressed me about Buck O'Neill and I've seen other interviews with him and things but even in this little clip that was his humor. Oh yes. So he had that piece where you know you know my wife died years ago and I'm single ladies and this is the end where he that's I that's why extended the clip that long where he goes you can stay here but I got to go to the bathroom. Hemer goes along. Here he is in this you know Hall of Fame situation he's done I got to go to the bathroom. How do you how do you understand humor as a way of being able to love in hard times what the value of that. Well I think humor is extremely important both in a physiological you know all these studies have been done to show that it makes you feel better if you laugh but I think it it helps leaven that us them dichotomy you know if you can just have a few laughs about some things and and humor often makes us understand that yeah we are all humans because we tend to laugh at the same sorts of things you know they've done studies on that too so yeah humor is absolutely essential I think in any walk of life we've got about a minute left here I you know I'm always taking note of people that they encourage me to expand expand my love beyond liking to yes this more discipline kind of love and Bucco Niel is kind of like one of the latest examples of that is there someone that stands out in your experience who would be an exemplar of that for your own life. Of well who can love beyond that you know I have a very good friend she's still alive is that okay I have a very good friend from college days who has that ability to just be herself and be so comfortable with everybody and accept everybody I have never seen her be the least bit harsh or rejecting and so I think about her a lot when I'm trying to work through some bad feelings about yeah it really does help to have people who are models and it's very she's very popular. People that are live are helpful yeah just have to be a dead person okay good well Zoe I mean thank you for being here I wish you the happiest and holiest of seasons here thank you and in the new year we'll hope to see you next year all right at your studio great thanks

this is Solta Kier on KOOP Austin 91.7 FM. I am your host and sacred chef.
Chuck Freeman at our new studios on 38th and Airport. Come on by and say howdy with us.
Check out. You will not believe if you've ever been to co-op. You will not believe one
of the finest studios in Austin, Texas. So we move now to the Hardy Fair portion of our
program. That's where we sit around with the soul food. Maybe watch a few dishes and
talk about how it went down and happy to have congregant Zoe Irene Van Zat in the studio
today. Just don't ask me to sing along with Buck. I'm surprised you could sing that well
at that. I had to know that. That's a nice one. That great. So what are your thoughts?
You said before we went on the air said I don't know anything about baseball but this
isn't about baseball. No it's not. It's about love. I see a lot of love in this studio.
Just the outpouring of the community to build this art to create this space. That's
really wonderful. Oh yeah. I mean so many people gave of time, money and energy and good
hard-edness and hard work. Yeah. It shows. It is wonderful studio. Especially if you've
seen the previous homes of KOOP as I have. You saw us on our previous fire trap, right?
Yep. Yeah. This is a big improvement. Yeah. Well glad you're able to be a part of it.
Thanks. So what do you think about Buck? I mean here's a guy who you know all of his life
you know lived under these racist conditions and even I knew the end was slided. But yeah
he still has this very positive loving attitude. I wish that under certain or similar circumstances
I would be as gracious I'm not sure I would be. Yeah. Well just as you look at your own
life to the extent you're able to do that what is it you're pulling from? Is it some
bullish system? You know when you are wrong or when you are slided. After the initial
hurt. Well you know I try to take the big picture. I try to look at the big picture and
I suppose my spiritual life is pretty much grounded in rationalism. So I try to look
at it from a rational point of view. Love is not always rational. I know certainly when
I've had an experience say of someone I thought we were really good friends and then
all of a sudden I'm no longer in their life. That is often a difficult situation and
corny as it sounds. I have to remember that little thing about you know some friends
are for a reason some friends are for a season etc. Okay so maybe this friend was just
for a season. To have that sort of universal love of mankind is difficult. I tend to love
people in the concrete and have a hard time with the concept of loving people abstract.
You know I love everybody. I don't think I can say that honestly. There are certain
people I love but just mankind in general. I'm not at that point yet where I can be that
loving. I mean that's something I need to work on. Well one of the things that I took
note of there with with the book and that's one of the reasons I read you may have recognized
those words from a first Corinthians 13 from your Christian days there is no way I
read that. One of the reasons I read that is kind of the top and the bottom of that
soul food piece is that he referred to the Yagapai love which is what St. Paul was referring
to there that godly unconditional love and I think that distinction that Boko Neal
made in his little speech clip there but I may not like him true but I love them. What
how do you understand that distinction? You know certainly I see that in the way I say
I look at politicians you know without naming names there are some of them that are very
difficult to love but you know or to like but you know I try to I try to stick to my
principle of well yes everybody has the right to their own opinion and if I have to and
I keep telling my students you know we have to watch the tendency humans have to separate
us into them and us because that's what causes conflict and horrible things and we have
to be able to see that other person as as human whether we like them whether we disagree
with them whatever the circumstances is we have to see them as a as another human or else
we get into that us in them situation that allows things like slavery and Holocaust and murder
and in Darfur and that sort of thing. So so that of course is going to one extreme. I hope
that that people can see some glimpse of love of concrete and abstract. I don't I'm not
sure everybody can do that you know I'm not sure everybody can open up and and just see
what love is is there in their life you know I in the last few months I really have been just
tremendous awakening to that it's been quite liberating. One of the things that impressed me about
Buck O'Neill and I've seen other interviews with him and things but even in this little clip
that was his humor. Oh yes. So he had that piece where you know you know my wife died years ago
and I'm single ladies and this is the end where he that's I that's why extended the clip that
long where he goes you can stay here but I got to go to the bathroom. Hemer goes along. Here he is
in this you know Hall of Fame situation he's done I got to go to the bathroom. How do you how do
you understand humor as a way of being able to love in hard times what the value of that. Well I
think humor is extremely important both in a physiological you know all these studies have been
done to show that it makes you feel better if you laugh but I think it it helps leaven that
us them dichotomy you know if you can just have a few laughs about some things and and humor often
makes us understand that yeah we are all humans because we tend to laugh at the same sorts of things
you know they've done studies on that too so yeah humor is absolutely essential I think in any
walk of life we've got about a minute left here I you know I'm always taking note of people that
they encourage me to expand expand my love beyond liking to yes this more discipline kind of love
and Bucco Niel is kind of like one of the latest examples of that is there someone
that stands out in your experience who would be an exemplar of that for your own life.
Of well who can love beyond that you know I have a very good friend she's still alive is that okay
I have a very good friend from college days who has that ability to just be herself and be so
comfortable with everybody and accept everybody I have never seen her be the least bit harsh
or rejecting and so I think about her a lot when I'm trying to work through some bad feelings about
yeah it really does help to have people who are models and it's very she's very popular. People
that are live are helpful yeah just have to be a dead person okay good well Zoe I mean thank you
for being here I wish you the happiest and holiest of seasons here thank you and in the new year
we'll hope to see you next year all right at your studio great thanks

note: Spiritual tribute to Buck O'Neil after his death at 91 in October 2006.
